channel.title: Concurrency on Chen Kai Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Concurrency on Chen Kai Blog

item.title: Databases (3): Transactions and Concurrency — ACID, Isolation Levels, and Locking
item.description:
Every application that handles money, inventory, or any state that matters eventually hits a concurrency bug. Two users buy the last item in stock. A bank transfer debits one account but crashes before crediting the other. A report reads half-updated data and produces nonsense numbers. Transactions exist to prevent these failures, and understanding how they work is non-negotiable for anyone building production systems.

What Is a Transaction?

A transaction is a group of operations that the database treats as a single unit. Either all operations succeed, or none of them do.

item.title: Python Engineering (6): Concurrency — Threads, Processes, and asyncio
item.description:
Your script downloads 100 files one at a time. Each download takes 2 seconds, mostly waiting for the server to respond. Total time: 200 seconds. But your CPU is idle for 99% of that time. You are paying for one thing (network latency) and wasting another (compute). Concurrency fixes this.

Python has three concurrency models, each designed for different problems. Pick the wrong one and your code is either still slow or riddled with race conditions. This article explains when to use each.
